coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical detection of cancer cells in human blood using folic acid and glutamic acid-functionalized graphene quantum dot-palladium@gold as redox probe with excellent electrocatalytic activity and target recognition
coredata.dc:creator: Ruiyi, Li
Tinling, Pu
Hongxia, Chu
Jinsong, Shi
Zaijun, Li
coredata.dc:description: Determination of circulating cancer cells in human blood is potential to be convenient diagnosis of different cancers. The study reports one strategy for synthesis of folic acid and glutamic acid-functionalized graphene quantum dot-palladium@gold (FA/Glu-GQD-Pd@Au). Firstly, FA/Glu-GQD was prepared by thermolysis of the mixture of citric acid, glutamic acid and folic acid. Then, it was used as the stabilizer and reducer for synthesis of FA/Glu-GQD-Pd@Au. The resulting hybrid was employed as one redox probe for construction of electrochemical sensing platform for cancer cells. The study reveals that the FA/Glu-GQD-Pd@Au offers one core@shell nanostructure. Pd nanocube as the core was covered by gold nanocrystal as the shell. For the detection, Pd@Au gives high electrocatalytic activity due to its unique structure as well as combination with FA/Glu-GQD. FA/Glu-GQD strongly binds with cancer cells to produce target recognition. FA/Glu-GQD occurs reversible redox reactions on the electrode surface and produces electrochemical signal for binding cancer cells. Pd@Au in situ catalyzes redox of FA/Glu-GQD and achieves to significant signal amplification. The sensor based on FA/Glu-GQD-Pd@Au exhibits ultrahigh sensitivity for detection of cancer cells. The differential pulse voltammetric peak current linearly reduces with the increase of cancer cells in the range of 3−1 × 105 HepG2 cells mL−1 with the detection limit of 2 cells mL−1 (S/N = 3). The proposed analytical method has been successfully applied in electrochemical detection of circulating cancer cells in human blood.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Voltammetry
Functionalized graphene quantum dots
palladium@gold
Cancer
Early diagnosis

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst vitae

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: State Administration of Foreign Experts Affairs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Science and Technology Department of Henan Province

Ministry of Education of the People's Republic of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the financial support from The National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2018YFC1603001 ), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21576115 ), Science and Technology Department of Henan Province of China (No. 192102210184 ) and MOE & SAFEA for the 111 Project ( B13025 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The procedure for synthesis of FA/Glu-GQD-Pd@Au.
The SEM image of Pd nanocubes (A) and TEM (B), HRTEM (C), EDS layered electron images (D) and elemental mappings of Au (E), Pd (F) and N (G) and merged element mapping of FA/Glu-GQD-Pd@Au.
The XRD patterns (A) and IR spectrum of FA/Glu-GQD-Pd@Au.
The fabrication of electrochemical sensing platform for cancer cells.

The CV curves (A) and Nyquist plots (B) of bare GCE (a) and FA/Glu-GQD-Pd@Au/GCE before (b) and after (c) incubated in 1.0 × 104 HepG2 cells mL−1 in 2 mM K2Fe(CN)6 in the PBS of pH 7.4 for 30 min. Insert in Fig. 5B: The enlarged Nyquist plot of FA/Glu-GQD-Pd@Au/GCE before incubated in the HepG2 cells.

Fluorescence images of the Pd@Au (left), Glu-GQD-Pd@Au (middle) and FA/Glu-GQD-Pd@Au (right) substrates after capturing HepG2 cells and the relative cell capture efficiencies for assay corresponding to different substrates.
CV (A) and DPV curves (B) of the sensor before (a) and after (b) incubated in the in 1.0 × 104 cells mL−1 HepG2 cell in the PBS buffer (pH 7.4).
DPV peak current change (ΔIp) of the sensor caused by the incubation in 1.0 × 104 cells mL−1 HepG2 cell in the PBS buffer (pH 7.4) for different times.

A: The DPV curves of the sensors incubated in 3, 10, 20, 50, 100, 200, 500, 1000, 2000, 50,000 and 100,000 HepG2 cancer cells mL−1 in the PBS buffer of pH7.4 (from top to bottom). B: The relationship curve of DPV current with logarithm of HepG2 cancer cell concentration.
Fluorescence images of FA/Glu-GQD-Pd@Au substrates after capturing HepG2 cells in fresh whole blood.
Fluorescence images of the Pd@Au substrate after captured L02 (A), HepG2 (B), HeLa (C) and A549 (D).
Comparison of the proposed sensor with other sensors for electrochemical detection of cancer cells.

row.entry: 0.1 M KCl and 5.0 mM K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] solution
0.01 M pH7.4 PBS, 10 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− and KCl

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The results for the detection of HepG2 cells in human blood samples.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical detection of cancer cells in human blood using folic acid and glutamic acid-functionalized graphene quantum dot-palladium@gold as redox probe with excellent electrocatalytic activity and target recognition

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Material Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The study reported one new concept for synthesis of folic acid and glutamic acid-functionalized graphene quantum dot-palladium@gold. The hybrid exhibits excellent electrocatalytic activity, redox behaviour and target recognition towards cancer cells. The sensor based on the hybrid has been successfully applied in electrochemical detetion of cancer cells in human blood.

Determination of circulating cancer cells in human blood is potential to be convenient diagnosis of different cancers. The study reports one strategy for synthesis of folic acid and glutamic acid-functionalized graphene quantum dot-palladium@gold (FA/Glu-GQD-Pd@Au). Firstly, FA/Glu-GQD was prepared by thermolysis of the mixture of citric acid, glutamic acid and folic acid. Then, it was used as the stabilizer and reducer for synthesis of FA/Glu-GQD-Pd@Au. The resulting hybrid was employed as one redox probe for construction of electrochemical sensing platform for cancer cells. The study reveals that the FA/Glu-GQD-Pd@Au offers one core@shell nanostructure. Pd nanocube as the core was covered by gold nanocrystal as the shell. For the detection, Pd@Au gives high electrocatalytic activity due to its unique structure as well as combination with FA/Glu-GQD. FA/Glu-GQD strongly binds with cancer cells to produce target recognition. FA/Glu-GQD occurs reversible redox reactions on the electrode surface and produces electrochemical signal for binding cancer cells. Pd@Au in situ catalyzes redox of FA/Glu-GQD and achieves to significant signal amplification. The sensor based on FA/Glu-GQD-Pd@Au exhibits ultrahigh sensitivity for detection of cancer cells. The differential pulse voltammetric peak current linearly reduces with the increase of cancer cells in the range of 3−1 × 105 HepG2 cells mL−1 with the detection limit of 2 cells mL−1 (S/N = 3). The proposed analytical method has been successfully applied in electrochemical detection of circulating cancer cells in human blood.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer has become one of the greatest hazards to human health [1]. Due to severe toxicity to normal cell and side effect, chemotherapy and radiotherapy after the operation often fail to cancer treatment and prolong patient survival. To date, early diagnosis still is the most effective method for cancer patients [2]. Present diagnosis is mainly based on the imaging of cancer tissues and the detection of cancer markers and circulating tumor cells in human blood. The tissue imaging gives a high reliability, but poor sensitivity makes it is difficult to use in cancer early diagnosis. Although the improved imaging techniques, including computer tomography (CT) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), can achieve to catch the sight of some small lesions, but these are highly up to machine operation technician and ability to uncontrollable errors [3]. The diagnosis with cancer markers is measuring the concentration of the corresponding cancer markers in human blood. The diagnosis always requires one complex sample treatment step for extraction and separation of tumor markers from human blood. This is possible to cause serious errors and omissions [4]. The determination of circulating tumor cells (CTCs) in human blood is potential to be convenient diagnosis of different cancers. Because CTCs come from escape of primary tumors and reproduce in other body parts, the CTCs are key pathway for cancer metastasis and eventually consume internal organs. Thus, the measuring CTCs from whole blood also provides a highly effective information through less invasive procedures and smaller false positive probabilities. However, the CTCs present a very low blood abundance. This requires an ultrahigh sensitivity of the analytical methods in the early diagnosis of cancers [5].

Many analytical techniques have been developed for detection of cancer cells such as immuno-fluorescent microscopy, nanoparticle-assisted cell imaging, fluorescent spectroscopy, surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) and electrochemical sensor [6]. The technique based on optical imaging needs long-term training, high expenditure, complicated pretreatment and long operation of hours [7]. The SERS is highly selective and sensitive to detect cancer cells, but the instability of detection signal restricts its applications in clinical diagnosis. Recently, the electrochemical method received a more attention to detection of cancer cells due to its high sensitivity, simplicity and inexpensive price [8]. To further improve the sensitivity, graphene (or graphene quantum dots) and gold have been widely investigated for electrochemical detection of CTCs due to their unique structure and characteristics. Graphene is one significant candidate with high electrical conductivity for reducing electrode impedance [9] and applied as the sensing material in clinic diagnosis [10], treatment of diseases [11], pesticide residue analysis [12] and environmental monitoring [13]. To achieve the specific capture of cancer cells, some antibodies and targeting molecules were introduced to fabricate graphene-based composite [14,15]. Mostly, targeting molecules were immobilized on the electrode surface by physical adsorption. However, the immobilized targeting molecules are easy to fall off from the electrode surface during the detection. This greatly effect on the repeatability of analytical method. To overcome the disadvantage, our group reported one method for synthesis of graphene aerogel microspheres from octadecyl amine and folic acid-functionalized graphene quantum dots [16]. The formed microsphere offers small size, open pores and high hydrophilicity. It has been applicable to electrochemically detect cancer cells [16]. Because of well-defined three-dimensional structure, high conductivity and covalent connections between graphene quantum dot and folic acid molecule, the graphene aerogel microspheres exhibits obviously enhanced sensitivity and stability. Different from graphene, gold nanocrystal can offer the special catalysis towards some redox molecules [17]. In addition, gold nanocrystal can also be used for immobilizing biomolecules through Au-S bond [18]. Based on the different functions of graphene and gold, several hybrids composing of gold nanoparticles and graphene quantum dots have been fabricated for electrochemical sensors [19–21]. These hybrids shows a much better catalytic activity compared with single graphene quantum dot and gold nanoparticles. More importantly, hybrid of graphene and gold creates a chance to satisfy multiple needs for the biosensor design. Although a great improvement has made, synthesis of gold-graphene quantum dot hybrid as the redox probe with excellent electrocatalytic activity and target recognition still faces a great challenge.

In the study, we report one strategy for synthesis of folic acid and glutamic acid-functionalized graphene quantum dot-palladium@gold hybrid (FA/Glu-GQD-Pd@Au). The resulting hybrid as the redox probe offers high electrocatalytic activity and target recognition towards cancer cells. The sensor based on FA/Glu-GQD-Pd@Au exhibits the advantage of sensitivity, selectivity and stability compared with other electrochemical sensors for cancer cells. The analytical method has been successfully applied in detection of cancer cells in human blood.

The palladium chloride aqueous solution (10 mM, 10 mL) and cetyltrimethylammonium chloride (CTAC) aqueous solution (3.6 wt.%, 5 mL) were added into 10 mL of ultrapure water in one vial. The vial was transferred into an air-bath at 30 °C. Added the potassium bromide (KBr) aqueous solution (1.0 mM, 0.7 mL) and let stand for 15 min. After added the potassium iodide (KI) aqueous solution (1.0 mM, 0.1 mL) and let stand for 15 min, added the FA/Glu-GQD solution (50 mg mL−1, 150 μL) and stirred for 40 min to growing Pd nanocubes. Followed by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 5 min and washing three times with ultrapure water. The obtained Pd nanocubes were re-dispersed in 10 mL of ultrapure water.

The chloroauric acid (HAuCl4) solution (10 mM, 5 mL) was mixed with the cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) aqueous solution (3.6 wt.%, 5 mL) in another vial. Added the FA/Glu-GQD aquous solution (50 mg mL−1, 100 μL) and heated at 95℃ until the color of the system turned to be dark red. kept the temperature for 6 min and then cooled to room temperature. Under stirring, rapidly injected the prepared Au seed solution into the as-prepared Pd nanocube solution. After that, continued to stir for 20 min and let stand for overnight in sequence. Followed by centrifugation at 8000 rpm and washing three times with ultrapure water. The collected FA/Glu-GQD-Pd@Au nanoparticles were re-dispersed in the Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.4, 1.0 mL) and stored at 4 °C until use.

The ITO glasses (5 mm × 5 mm square pieces) as the carrier of FA/Glu-GQD-Pd@Au were used for cell imaging. The FA/Glu-GQD-Pd@Au was immobilized on the ITO glasses by using the same method for the sensor preparation. To capture cells from 3.0 mL of the cell suspension (1.0 × 104 cell mL−1), the pre-treated ITO glasses were placed at 37 °C in 5 % CO2 atmosphere for 60 min. Next, the PBS buffer (pH 7.4) was used to wash the pre-treated ITO glasses three times to remove the specific adsorbate. To observe more, a paraformaldehyde solution was used to fix the cancer cells, which were captured by FA/Glu-GQD-Pd@Au on the ITO surface. These cells were stained with 4,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) and and then observed by Olympus fluorescence microscope.

The detection of HepG2 cell was taken by immersing the sensor in a HepG2 cell solution or sample solution for 30 min. Then, the sensor was carefully taken out and gently washed by distilled water to remove the redundant cell. Then, the sensor was put into the PBS buffer (pH 7.4) with or without 2 mM K4Fe(CN)6 for cyclic voltammetric measurement (CV) and differential pulse voltammetric measurement (DPV).

Synthesis of FA/Glu-GQD-Pd@Au includes three steps (shown in Fig. 1 ). The first step is to make FA/Glu-GQD by hydrolysis of the mixture of citric acid, folic acid and glutamic acid. For the synthesis, citric acid was used as the carbon source for forming graphene sheets. Due to unique molecule structure and chemical properties, citric acid is easy to convert into graphene sheets by intermolecular condensation and subsequent carbonization [22]. Folic acid was used as the functional reagent for introducing folic acid groups into the edge of graphene sheets. Folic acid is commonly used targeting molecule for cancer targeting therapy [23] and diagnosis [24]. Thus, the introduction of folic acid gives FA/Glu-GQD the ability to the target recognition towards. Glutamic acid was used as the functional reagent for introducing glutamic acid groups into the edge of graphene sheets. Glutamic acid is an electroactive substance that can be electrochemically oxidized and reduced [25]. The introduction of glutamic acid gives the FA/Glu-GQD high electroactivity. By the p-π electron conjugation between nitrogen atom in the amide groups and the π-π electron conjugation between carbonyl in the glutamic acid and graphene sheet, the electroactivity of FA/Glu-GQD is greatly improved. The as-designed FA/Glu-GQD was synthesized by one-step simple hydrolysis. The study demonstrates that the choosing an appropriate reaction temperature is important to obtain an ideal FA/Glu-GQD redox probe. Under the temperature, citric acid can change into graphene sheets, but folic acid and glutamic acid still do not decompose. The result indicates that the use of a 180 °C for 3 h can realize the above aim. In addition, the morphology and size of FA/Glu-GQD was characterized by TEM analysis. Fig. s1 shows that FA/Glu-GQD is composed of small graphene sheets with the average size of 4.5 nm. The interplanar spacing of 0.24 nm in the insert in Fig. s1 is corresponded to the (002) crystal plane of graphene.

The second step is to make Pd nanocubes by reduction of PdCl2 in the presence of CTAC, KBr, KI and FA/Glu-GQD [26]. In the step, CTAC was served as a soft template for formation of Pd nanocubes. FA/Glu-GQD was used as the reducer and stabilizer to reduce Pd+ into Pd°, finally leading to the formation of Pd naocubes. Both Br− and I− are ligands towards Pd+, which can combine with Pd2+ to form stable Pd complex. The formation these complex will largely reduce the reduction potential of Pd+. This results in a faster reduction process and produces more Pd nanocubes. As a result, growth of Pd nanocubes can complete at a relatively low temperature (30 °C).

The third step is to fabricate FA/Glu-GQD-Pd@Au hybrid by using the modified seed growth method [26]. During synthesis of Au seeds, FA/Glu-GQD was used as the stabilizer and reducer. When the HAuCl4 aqeous solution was mixed with the FA/Glu-GQD aqueous solution, the color deepens rapidly and eventually turns to wine red. This verifies that FA/Glu-GQD can reduce Au3+ into Au0, finally results in the formation of Au seeds. During the growth of Au seeds, the FA/Glu-GQDs were immobilized to the superficies of Au seeds by the coordination bonds between Au+ and some functional groups such as -NH2, −OH and −COOH in the FA/Glu-GQD. Then, formation of FA/Glu-GQD-Pd@Au was prepared by rapidly adding the resulting Au seed into the Pd nanocube solution. In the presence of FA/Glu-GQD and CTAB, gold nanoparticles were grown in the surface of Pd nanocubes with the reduction of Pd+. The Pd nanocube merges into the whole gold shell for the formation of Au nanoparticles, which shows a palladium-gold core@shell nanostructure.

The as-synthesized FA/Glu-GQD-Pd@Au was characterized by SEM, TEM, XRD and IR spectrum. Fig. 2 presents the SEM image of Pd nanocubes and TEM and high resolution TEM (HRTEM), EDS layered electron images and elemental mappings of Au, Pd and N as well as the merged element mapping of FA/Glu-GQD-Pd@Au. It can be seen from Fig. 2A that Pd nanoparticles shows cube-like nanostructure with side length of about 150 nm. Different from Pd nanocubes, FA/Glu-GQD-Pd@Au nanoparticles give sphere-like morphology with an average particle size of 250 nm. Pd nanocubes is in the central position of FA/Glu-GQD-Pd@Au and while Au nanocrystals are distributed around Pd nanocubes, indicating one core@shell nanostructure. On the HRTEM image there are two interplanar spacings of 0.21 nm and 0.24 nm. These are corresponded to (111) crystal planes of Pd nanocube and Au nanocrystals, respectively. The elemental mapping analysis reveals that Au atoms are mainly gathered on the surface of FA/Glu-GQD-Pd@Au as the shell. All Pd atoms are distributed the inside of nanoparticles as the core. The above results prove the core-shell nanostructure of FA/Glu-GQD-Pd@Au again. Due to perfect hybrid of FA/Glu-GQD and Pd@Au, nitrogen (N) element is uniformly distributed within whole nanoparticles. Fig. 3 shows the XRD patterns and IR spectrum of FA/Glu-GQD-Pd@Au. On the XRD patterns there are eight peaks at 38.24°, 40.12°, 44.38°, 46.62°, 64.50°, 68.02°,77.66° and 81.98°. The patterns at 38.24°, 44.38°, 64.50° and 77.66° are corresponded to the (311), (222), (220), (200) and (111) planes of Au crystals. The patterns at 40.12°, 46.62°, 68.02° and 81.98° are corresponded to the (311), (222), (220), (200) and (111) planes of Pd crystals. All XRD patterns in Fig. 3A are sharp and strong, indicating high crystallinity. Due to weak crystallization of FA/Glu-GQD, characteristic XRD peak of graphene at 26° is quite weak even cannot be seen in the XRD patterns [27]. On the IR spectrum there are seven infrared absorption peaks and bands. The symmetric stretching vibration of NH and OH bonds presents as the IR absorption band at about 3442 cm−1. The absorption peak of 3000 cm−1 is the IR absorption caused by symmetric stretching vibration of the saturated C–H bonds. The IR absorption peak at 1691 cm−1 is due to the stretch vibration of CO bond. The stretching vibration of CO bond contributes to the IR absorption peak at 1386 cm−1. As Pd and Au nanocrystals have no significant IR absorption from 3800 to 540 cm−1, the above IR absorption bands and peaks are mainly attributed to FA/Glu-GQD. The result proves again the existence of FA/Glu-GQD in the FA/Glu-GQD-Pd@Au.

The sensing scheme for electrochemical detection of cancer cells is presented at Fig. 4 . The sensor fabrication only needs one-step simple immobilization of FA/Glu-GQD-Pd@Au on the surface of glass carbon electrode (GCE), in which chitosan was employed as the adhesive to form highly sensitive and selective sensing interface. In the sensing scheme, FA/Glu-GQD-Pd@Au as the sensing material plays three functions. The first function is to realize capturing cancer cells. Due to the introduction of FA, FA/Glu-GQD can strongly bind with cancer cells to realize the target recognition. The second function is to produce electrochemical response for binding cancer cells. The FA/Glu-GQD can occur reversible oxidation and reduction on the electrode surface owing to high electroactivity of Glu groups [25]. Thus FA/Glu-GQD can be used as the redox probe, producing electrochemical detection signal for cancer cells. The third function is to amplify the detection signal. Due to excellent electrocatalytic activity, Pd@Au in situ catalyzes oxidation and reduction of FA/Glu-GQD and results in a significant signal amplification. As a result, the sensing platform based on FA/Glu-GQD-Pd@Au is conducive to achieving high selective and sensitive detection of cancer cells.

To understand electrochemical property of FA/Glu-GQD-Pd@Au, the cyclic voltammetric (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectrum (EIS) performances of bare GCE and FA/Glu-GQD-Pd@Au/GCE before and after incubated in 1.0 × 104 cells mL−1 HepG2 cells were assessed in 2 mM K2Fe(CN)6 in the PBS of pH 7.4. Fig. 5 indicates that all CV curves offer one pair of redox peaks. This is mainly attributed to oxidation and reduction of K2Fe(CN)6 on the electrode surface. Compared with bare GCE, FA/Glu-GQD-Pd@Au/GCE gives a higher CV peak current and a lower electron transfer impedance (Rct). FA/Glu-GQD-Pd@Au presents lots of catalytic sites due to deep combination of gold and palladium with FA/Glu-GQD as the linker. These lead to an increase in the electrocatalytic activity and electron transfer due to their synergistic effects. However, the peak current was sharply dropping down and an obvious increase in the Rct after incubated with cancer cells. The folic acid group as targeting site capture the cancer cells and assembled on the surface of the sensor. The captured cells partly blocks the channels between the electrode and the electrolyte for electron/ion transport. These factors lead to a slower electrode reaction process, indicating a reduced CV current and an increased Rct value.

To test the capturing ability of the proposed sensor towards cancer cells, three kinds of nanomaterials, including Pd@Au, Glu-GQD-Pd@Au and FA/Glu-GQD-Pd@Au, were used for construction of the electrochemical sensing platform for cancer cells by using the same procedure. Firstly, he modified ITO glasses were incubated in a 1.0 × 104 cells mL−1 HepG2 cell in the PBS buffer (pH 7.4) for 30 min and then washed with the PBS buffer (pH 7.4) to remove the specific adsorbate. Then, the captured cancer cells on the ITO glasses were stained by DAPI and taken by fluorescent microscope. The results were shown in Fig. 6 . The fluorescent image of Pd@Au offers nearly no signal with the cell capture efficiency of 2.7 %, verifying that Pd@Au does not effectively capture the cancer cells. When Pd@Au was replaced by Glu-GQD-Pd@Au, the cell capture efficiency increases to 21.3 %. The value is obviously higher than that of Pd@Au. This is because the Glu-GQD contains some biophilic groups. The existence of these functional groups makes Glu-GQD-Pd@Au can capture the cancer cells via various van der Waals forces and resulted in an obviously improved cell capture efficiency. Compared with Pd@Au and Glu-GQD-Pd@Au, FA/Glu-GQD-Pd@Au offers a much higher cell capture efficiency of 62.8 %. This is because the folic acid groups on FA/Glu-GQD-Pd@Au enhance the connect with the cancer cells, contributing to an increased cell capture efficiency. The integration of FA/Glu-GQD with Pd@Au result in a larger specific surface. The above result demonstrate that the sensor can capture cancer cells with an excellent cell capture efficiency.

FA/Glu-GQD-Pd@Au has highly electrochemical activity due to introduction of Glu that can operate the redox reactions on the surface of electrode. The characteristic determines that it can be used as one redox probe for electrochemical detection of cancer cells, producing a sensitive and selective electrochemical response. To form the above suggest, CV and DPV behaviors of the as-prepared sensor were investigated before and after incubated in 1.0 × 104 cells mL−1 HepG2 cell in the PBS buffer (pH 7.4). The results were shown in Fig. 7 . There are one pair of reversible redox peaks at -0.12/0.19 V in the PBS buffer. This is mainly attributed to the redox of FA/Glu-GQD-Pd@Au on the electrode surface. Because the folic acid groups in the sensor can bind with cancer cells, the incubation results in the immobilization of cancer cells on the electrode surface. The poor electrical conductivity of the captured cancer cells slows down the rate of electrode reactions. In addition, the existence of these cancer cells on the electrode surface partly blocks the electron/ion transport between the electrode and the electrolyte. The above factors bring an obvious increase in the peak currents on the CV and DPV curves. Fig. 7B also shows that compared with CV, the DPV has a much more sensitive response to cancer cells. Therefore, the DPV technology was selected to determine the cancer cells as the electrochemical method.

To verify the feasibility for cancer cell detection, the as-proposed sensors were incubated in different concentrations of HepG2 cancer cells for 30 min and then their DPV curves were measured by using the same procedure. During the process, a known concentration of HepG2 cancer cells were added to whole blood sample in the ranging from 1.0 to 106 cells mL−1. The results were shown in Fig. 8A. The relation between the peak current on the DPV curve and the cell concentration has a declining trend. After the concentration of HepG2 cells increased, the peak current will rapidly drop down. Further, Fig. 8B presents the relationship curve between the DPV peak currents and the corresponding cell concentration. When the cell concentration is in the range from 3 to 105 cells mL−1, there is a good linear relationship between the Ip value and the cell concentration. The linear regression equation was: I p = 646.65LOG[HepG2, cells mL−1]+6195.9, in which the Ip of the peak current is in nA, the C of HepG2 cells concentration is in cells mL−1 and the statistically significant correlation coefficient was 0.992. The limit of detection was found to be 2 cells mL−1, indicating high sensitivity. The sensitivity is much better that of other sensors for detection of cancer cells reported in Table 1 . Such an ultrahigh sensitivity is mainly due to excellent electrocatalytic activity and target recognition towards cancer cells of FA/Glu-GQD-Pd@Au.

The ability to capture HepG2 cells from fresh whole blood samples was tested. The HepG2 cells of 1 × 104 mL−1 was stained with 1,1-dioctadecyl-3,3,3,3-tetramethylindodicarbocyanine (DID) before adding into the human whole blood. The results were shown in Fig. 10 . After incubating for 30 min, all the captured cells were stained with 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) on the FA/Glu-GQD-Pd@Au. Based on the fluorescence images, almost all cells present both red and blue fluorescence, which can be indicated as the HepG2 cells. The red blood cells and white blood cells in the human whole blood samples were only stained with DAPI, which shows blue fluorescence. As a result, the FA/Glu-GQD-Pd@Au can specifically capture the cancer cells in the fresh whole blood samples.

The specificity of FA/Glu-GQD-Pd@Au for cancer cell capture was verified by comparing HepG2, HeLa and A549 cancer cell lines as one model cancer cells with the L02 normal cell lines as negative. The PBS buffer (pH 7.4) with 1.0 × 104 cells mL−1 of different cell lines was carried out for the measurement of the cell capture with FA/Glu-GQD-Pd@Au. Fig. 11 indicates that the capture rate of L02 by FA/Glu-GQD-Pd@Au was only 6.7 %, which was much lower than that of other cancer cells, HepG2 (62.8 %), HeLa (51.4 %) and A549 (66.2 %). The overexpressed folate receptor enhances the ability to capture the HeLa, A549 and HepG2 cancer cell lines.

The serum samples of healthy female adults were collected for assessing the performance of this electrochemical sensors to HepG2 cell recovery assays. The recovery rates are shown in Table 2 from 95.1–102.6 %, which is clarified that this method can still achieve the desired effect even in complex actual samples.

The study demonstrated synthesis of folic acid and glutamic acid-functionalized graphene quantum dot-palladium@gold hybrid and its use as the redox probe for electrochemical detection of cancer cells. Folic acid in the hybrid offers good targeting ability to cancer cells. The combination of graphene sheets with glutamic acid realizes a highly electrochemical activity. It can carry out the reversible redox reaction on the electrode surface and produce a sensitive electrochemical response to cancer cells. The palladium@gold in the hybrid gives high electrocatalytic activity due to its unique structure as well as hybrid of FA/Glu-GQD and Pd@Au. It can in situ catalyze the redox reactions of the graphene quantum dots, producing significant signal amplification. The sensor based on the hybrid provide ultrahigh selectivity, sensitivity and stability for electrochemical detection of cancer cells.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from The National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2018YFC1603001), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21576115), Science and Technology Department of Henan Province of China (No. 192102210184) and MOE & SAFEA for the 111 Project (B13025).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2020.127709.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: In vitro biocompatibility of surface-modified porous alumina particles for HepG2 tumor cells: toward early diagnosis and targeted treatment
Precision diagnosis and treatment of liver cancer in China
Copper (II) oxide nanozyme based electrochemical cytosensor for high sensitive detection of circulating tumor cells in breast cancer
Porous graphene oxide enhanced aptamer specific circulating-tumor-cell sensing interface on light addressable potentiometric sensor: clinical application and simulation
Cellular interface supported toehold strand displacement cascade for amplified dual-electrochemical signal and its application for tumor cell analysis
Rapid, label-free, and highly sensitive detection of cervical cancer with fluorescence lifetime imaging microscopy
An electrochemical nanobiosensor for plasma mirna-155, based on graphene oxide and gold nanorod, for early detection of breast cancer
A graphene functionalized electrochemical aptasensor for selective label-free detection of cancer cells
Highly sensitive biosensor based on graphene- poly(3-aminobenzoic acid) modified electrodes and porous-hollowed-silver-gold nanoparticle labelling for prostate cancer detection
A graphene-based electrochemical device with thermoresponsive microneedles for diabetes monitoring and therapy
Application of graphene for preconcentration and highly sensitive stripping voltammetric analysis of organophosphate pesticide
Enhancing the sensitivity of hexachlorobenzene electrochemical sensor based on nitrogen–doped graphene
Targeting and sensing of some cancer cells using folate bioreceptor functionalized nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
An ultrasensitive sandwich-type electrochemical immunosensor for the determination of skbr-3 breast cancer cell using rgo-tpa/fehcfnano labeled anti-hct as a signal tag
Electrochemical sensor for detection of cancer cell based on folic acid and octadecylamine-functionalized graphene aerogel microspheres
Electrochemical determination of hydrogen peroxide on a gold nanoparticle–nitrogen-doped graphene glassy carbon electrode
Electrochemical sensor for arsenite detection using graphene oxide assisted generation of prussian blue nanoparticles as enhanced signal label
Ultrafast synthesis of gold/proline- functionalized graphene quantum dots and its use for ultrasensitive electrochemical detection of p-acetamidophenol
Electrochemical determination of acetaminophen using a glassy carbon electrode modified with a hybrid material consisting of graphene aerogel and octadecylamine- functionalized carbon quantum dots
Synthesis of gold-palladium nanowaxberries/dodecylamine- functionalized graphene quantum dots-graphene micro-aerogel for voltammetric determination of peanut allergen Ara h 1
Glowing graphene quantum dots and carbon dots: properties, syntheses, and biological applications
Delivery of doxorubicin for human cervical carcinoma targeting therapy by folic acid-modified selenium nanoparticles
In vivo examination of folic acid-conjugated gold-silica nanohybrids as contrast agents for localized tumor diagnosis and biodistribution
Electrochemical synthesis of (poly)dimethoxyaniline on glassy carbon electrodes and their applications in the detection of L- and D-glutamic acids
Synthesis of gold-palladium nanowaxberries/dodecylamine- functionalized graphene quantum dots-graphene micro-aerogel for voltammetric determination of peanut allergen Ara h 1
Graphene oxide and reduced graphene oxide studied by the XRD, TEM and electron spectroscopy methods
Development of clay-protein based composite nanoparticles modified single-used sensor platform for electrochemical cytosensing application
An ultrasensitive electrochemical sensor based on cotton carbon fiber composites for the determination of superoxide anion release from cells
Conductive polymer coated scaffold to integrate 3D cell culture with electrochemical sensing
CuO/WO3 nanoparticles decorated graphene oxide nanosheets with enhanced peroxidase-like activity for electrochemical cancer cell detection and targeted therapeutics
Integration of fluorescence imaging and electrochemical biosensing for both qualitative location and quantitative detection of cancer cells
Peptide nanoparticles (PNPs) modified disposable platform for sensitive electrochemical cytosensing of DLD-1 cancer cells
Enhanced electrochemical sensing of leukemia cells using drug/lipid co-immobilized on the conducting polymer layer
Efficient electrochemical detection of cancer cells on in situ surface-functionalized MoS2 nanosheets

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Li Ruiyi is currently associate professor of School of Pharmaceutical Science, Jiangnan University, China. She received his master degree from the Birmingham University, The United Kingdom, 2014. She received her doctor degree from Jiangnan University, China, in 2019. Her research interest focus on synthesis of graphene-based nanomaterials and their application in pharmaceutical analysis and drug delivery.

Pu Tinling is one doctoral student of Professor Shi Jinsong in School of Pharmaceutical Science, Jiangnan University, China. Her research interest is development of biomaterials and applications in biomedicine.

Chu Hongxia is currently one master student of Professor Li Zaijun in School of Chemical and Materials Engineering, Jiangnan University, China. Her research interest is synthesis of nanomaterials and their application in electrochemical chemistry.

Shi Jinsong is one Professor of School of Pharmaceutical Science, Jiangnan University, China. He is associate dean in School of Pharmaceutical Science, Jiangnan University, China. He received his doctor degree from Jiangnan University, China, in 2005. He is author of more than 100 papers and reviewer of more than 20 journals. His current research interests are Biomanufacturing of pharmaceutical chemicals and green manufacturing of pharmaceuticals, Separation, preparation and functional evaluation of bioactive substances, Excavation, Screening and Application of Bioenzymes.

Li Zaijun is one professor of School of Chemical and Materials Engineering, Jiangnan University, China. He received his doctor degree from Jiangnan University, China, in 2004. He is author of more than 200 papers and reviewer of more than 40 journals. His current research interests are synthesis of graphene-based nanomaterials and their applications in sensing, electrocatalysis and energy storage/conversion devices.
